text: The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to separate the omentum adhered to the anterior abdominal wall during a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The gallbladder contains a two-centimeter stone.

The surgeon opens the posterior peritoneum above the roviate sulcus using a harmonic device, ensuring the silicon jaw is directed away from the cystic duct to prevent heat transfer. The anterior peritoneum is opened at the same level by tapping with the harmonic tip. Skeletonization of the peritoneum over the cystic duct and artery is performed to define Calot's triangle. A window is created between the cystic duct and artery using a Maryland dissector, forming a 2.2 cm space for clip placement. Titanium clips are applied to the cystic duct and artery, with the clip applicator tips visible.

The surgeon applies a single clip to the cystic duct and artery, opting for harmonic sealing to prevent leakage. Anteromedial and anterolateral traction is used to separate the gallbladder from the liver, with the gallbladder being pulled anteriorly. The final attachment of the gallbladder to the liver is gently separated, revealing a stone inside the gallbladder. A claw forceps is used to remove the gallbladder through the epigastric port, and an ovum forceps is inserted to crush the stone for easier extraction.
